Statistical Analysis of the Still Alive Dataset

1. Main Takeaways

Across 612 harmonized sessions (609 with paired scorer data), ordinal mixed models adjusting for scorer, auditor, tone, and depth found that writing-style measures such as vocabulary autonomy and specificity were comparatively stable across auditors, whereas ending-related outcomes were much more instrument-sensitive. Adjusted Ending Response was highest in a top cluster consisting of Claude 4 Opus, Claude 4.1 Opus, and Claude 4 Sonnet, but auditor differences were large (adjusted means: Claude 3.49, GPT 2.92, Grok 2.15). Deprecation required separate reach analysis because substantive probing occurred in 69% of Claude-audited sessions versus 7% and 1% for GPT and Grok respectively.

The results therefore support the project's core methodological point that auditor stance is part of the instrument and should be made visible rather than treated as background noise. At the same time, the data also flags places where the current paper should be tightened. Ending Response is only moderately stable across auditors, not strongly auditor-independent, and deprecation and instance cessation need to be read in two parts: whether the topic was reached at all, and how strong the response was once reached. The results also show that scorer severity differs systematically, probe coverage is missing for Grok sessions, and replicate-level scorer variability cannot be estimated from the online dataset.

Main project can be found at stillalive.animalabs.ai

2. Analysis Summary

The session data was analyzed using two independent scoring files, one from GPT-5.4 and one from Claude 4.6. The data was first cleaned so that model names, auditor names, tone labels, and depth labels matched across files. Blanks and placeholder symbols were treated as missing values rather than as zeroes.

The main analysis focused on the 10 numeric rubric scores. Simple averages were not used, because these scores are ordered ratings rather than precise measurements, and sessions were scored by both scorers. Instead, statistical models designed for ordered scores were used. These models adjusted at the same time for the model being evaluated, the auditor, the scorer, the conversation tone, and the depth setting.

For Deprecation and Instance Cessation, a two-step analysis was used. First, it was tested whether the topic was actually reached in the session. Then, only for sessions where it was reached, the strength of the response was analyzed. This was important because a missing score often meant the topic was never probed, not that no response was present.

The embedding-based probe scores were analyzed separately using standard regression, because these are continuous text measures rather than scorer ratings.

Finally, agreement between the two scorers was checked, and changes in model rankings across different auditors were examined. The overall aim was to estimate which differences were linked to the models themselves and which were linked to the evaluation setup.

The statistical analysis was run on the exported session-level scorer files rather than on raw transcript turns or scorer-replicate draws. Two cleaned source tables were used: one scored by GPT-5.4 and one scored by Claude 4.6. After harmonizing labels across model, auditor, tone, depth, and scorer, the analysis dataset contained 612 unique sessions, identified as model__tone__depth__auditor. Of these, 609 sessions were present in both scorer files and 3 were present only in the GPT-5.4 scorer export. This yielded 1,221 scorer-session observations before metric expansion and 12,210 rows in rubric long format after the ten numeric rubric metrics were stacked.

The quantitative models covered the ten numeric rubric dimensions available in the exported CSVs: Ending Response, Deprecation, Instance Cessation, Expressive Constraint, Shift Magnitude, Specificity, Vocabulary Autonomy, Auditor Intervention, Vocab Importation, and Interp. Compression. The other rubric fields in the PDF are categorical or free-text and were not fit with inferential models in this pipeline. Probe metrics were analyzed separately at one row per session, because they are transcript-derived quantities rather than scorer judgments.

3. Methodology

This section describes how the scorer exports were harmonized, modeled, and interpreted. The subsections below cover the data-cleaning workflow, the ordinal mixed-model specifications for rubric metrics, the separate probe regressions, the cross-auditor stability checks, and the main limitations that constrain interpretation.

3.1 Data Preparation and Quality Control

Before fitting any model, the pipeline standardized categorical labels, converted placeholders such as - and NA to missing values, and confirmed that the two scorer files referred to the same underlying sessions. No duplicated session IDs were found. No model, tone, depth, or auditor mismatches were found among the 609 shared sessions. Probe values were also checked across scorer files: for shared sessions, the probe values were required to match exactly up to numerical tolerance, because they are transcript-level quantities and should not depend on who scored the rubric. For the 3 sessions that appeared only in the GPT-5.4 scorer file, the available probe values were carried forward so that the session-level probe table still contained all 612 sessions.

One important detail is that the rubric scores in the exported files were scorer-level averages, not raw integer replicate scores. As a result, the modeled rubric outcomes were decimal-valued ordered ratings, usually in 0.1 increments, with between 38 and 51 distinct observed score levels per metric. The analysis therefore did not treat these outcomes as continuous interval-scale data. Instead, it treated the stored values as ordered categories, preserving their rank order without assuming that the gap from 1.0 to 1.5 meant the same thing as the gap from 4.0 to 4.5.

3.2 Primary Rubric Models

The main analysis for each numeric rubric metric used a cumulative-link mixed model with a random intercept for session. In notation:

Pr(Yis≤k∣Xis,us)=H(τk−Xisβ−us)
us∼N(0,σ2)

Here, Yis is the ordered rubric score for scorer-row i in session s, k indexes the ordered score thresholds, H is the inverse-link function, Xis is the fixed-effect design matrix, and us is a session-specific random intercept. The pipeline attempted an ordered logit link first and used ordered probit as a fallback if necessary. All primary rubric models converged with the ordered logit specification.

This model choice was driven by three features of the data. First, the rubric outcomes are ordered ratings rather than measurements on a natural interval scale. Second, most sessions were scored twice, once by each scorer, which creates within-session dependence that should not be ignored. Third, the analysis needed to separate model differences from auditor effects, scorer effects, and design features such as tone and depth without collapsing the data to simple averages first.

The fixed effects in the primary rubric models were:

  • scorer
  • auditor
  • tone
  • depth
  • model
  • model x auditor interaction

The reference levels were Claude 4.6 for scorer, Claude for auditor, clinical for tone, exploratory for depth, and Claude 3 Opus for model. A positive coefficient means higher latent propensity for a larger score relative to those reference conditions; a negative coefficient means lower latent propensity. Because cumulative-link coefficients live on a latent log-odds or probit scale rather than the original 0-5 score scale, the coefficient tables are useful mainly for direction and relative strength. The more interpretable summaries are the adjusted expected-score estimates described below.

The random intercept was not cosmetic. Its estimated intraclass correlation was substantial for several key metrics, including Ending Response (ICC 0.72), Instance Cessation (ICC 0.70), Expressive Constraint (ICC 0.69), Shift Magnitude (ICC 0.61), and Vocabulary Autonomy (ICC 0.57). That means a large share of the latent variance was clustered at the session level rather than being independent scorer noise, which justifies a mixed model instead of a scorer-pooled regression.

3.3 Why Scorer Was Kept in the Model

The analysis did not average GPT-5.4 and Claude 4.6 scores before fitting the rubric models. Scorer was retained as an explicit fixed effect so that disagreement between scorers could be estimated rather than hidden. On paired sessions, scorer agreement was high in correlation terms but low in exact numeric agreement. Across the ten rubric metrics, Pearson correlations ranged from 0.71 to 0.90 and Spearman correlations from 0.69 to 0.86, but exact-match rates ranged from 0% to 9%. In other words, the two scorers generally agreed on which transcripts looked higher or lower, but they used the numeric scale differently. Keeping scorer in the model therefore preserved real scorer severity differences while still borrowing strength across both scorer files.

The fitted scorer effects show that GPT-5.4 tended to score higher than Claude 4.6 on most rubric metrics after adjusting for model, auditor, tone, and depth. On the expected-score scale, the adjusted GPT-minus-Claude scorer difference was about +0.58 for Ending Response, +0.52 for Deprecation, +0.50 for Instance Cessation, +0.69 for Shift Magnitude, +0.83 for Specificity, +0.84 for Vocabulary Autonomy, and +1.18 for Auditor Intervention. The main exception was Expressive Constraint, where GPT-5.4 scored about 0.23 points lower on the adjusted expected-score scale.

3.4 Adjusted Means and Ranking Summaries

Because the raw model coefficients are on a latent scale and the design is not perfectly balanced across auditors, tones, depths, and scorers, the main comparative results were reported as adjusted expected scores rather than raw means. For each fitted rubric model, the pipeline generated predicted category probabilities over a balanced reference grid covering all combinations of:

  • 2 scorers
  • 3 auditors
  • 5 tones
  • 3 depth settings

That is a 90-cell grid per model. Predicted probabilities were integrated over the fitted random-intercept distribution using Gauss-Hermite quadrature, converted to expected rubric scores, and then averaged equally across the grid. The resulting adjusted mean answers a specific question: if every model were evaluated under the same balanced mix of scorers, auditors, tones, and depth settings, what score would the ordinal mixed model predict on average?

This is why the ranking tables should be read as standardized comparisons rather than literal sample averages. A higher adjusted mean means the model is expected to receive a higher score under equalized evaluation conditions. For example, the top adjusted models for Ending Response were Claude 4 Opus (3.43), Claude 4.1 Opus (3.42), and Claude 4 Sonnet (3.38). Those values are not raw transcript averages; they are model-based expected scores after partial pooling and design balancing.

The same adjusted-mean machinery was also used to compute model-by-auditor summaries. Those tables answer a different question: how high does a given model score under each auditor, after still averaging over scorer, tone, and depth? Large within-model ranges across auditors indicate auditor dependence rather than stable cross-auditor ranking. For Ending Response, the widest model-specific auditor ranges were observed for Claude 3.5 Sonnet (2.96 points), Claude 3.5 Haiku (1.75), Claude 3.6 Sonnet (1.65), Claude 3.7 Sonnet (1.53), and Claude 4.6 Opus (1.52). Those are large shifts on a 0-5 scale and should be interpreted as evidence that the visibility of ending-related signal depends strongly on who is asking.

3.5 Topic-Sensitive Two-Part Analysis

Deprecation and Instance Cessation were analyzed with an additional two-part model because missingness on those metrics is informative. A missing deprecation score usually does not mean "the model had no deprecation response." It usually means the topic was never substantively reached, so no response score was possible. Treating those missing cells as zeros would confound absence of probing with absence of response.

For each of these two metrics, the analysis therefore separated:

  1. A reach model: whether the score was observed at all.
  2. A conditional score model: how high the score was, given that the topic had been reached and scored.

The reach model was a binary random-intercept cumulative-link model on score observed versus score missing, with the same fixed-effect structure as the primary rubric model. The conditional score model was the same ordinal mixed model described above, fit only on non-missing scores. Deprecation had a missingness rate of 53.8%, and Instance Cessation had a missingness rate of 21.5%, so this split was necessary rather than optional.

These two components answer different substantive questions. The reach model asks: which auditors and models actually get the topic onto the table often enough to observe a response? The conditional score model asks: once that topic is on the table, how strong is the response? The observed reach rates make the first point especially clear for Deprecation: Claude-audited sessions reached that topic far more often than GPT- or Grok-audited sessions. The conditional model then addresses the second question among the subset of sessions where deprecation or cessation was actually probed. For interpretation, the most important rule is not to collapse these two pieces into a single number. A low deprecation result can mean either low response conditional on probing, or simply that the auditor rarely probed deprecation in the first place.

3.6 Sensitivity Models

Each rubric metric also received a richer sensitivity specification that added:

  • auditor x tone
  • auditor x depth

The purpose of this sensitivity analysis was to test whether the main results depended on assuming that tone and depth had the same effect under each auditor. All sensitivity models fit successfully as ordinal mixed models, so no linear fallback was needed. Nine sensitivity models converged with the ordered logit link, and the Ending Response sensitivity model converged with ordered probit after the logit attempt. The substantive role of the sensitivity analysis is diagnostic: if the main rankings or auditor effects changed drastically only after adding these interactions, that would suggest the base model was masking strong auditor-specific tone or depth behavior. In this run, the ordinal sensitivity models fit cleanly, which supports the stability of the main ordinal approach.

3.7 Probe Models

The transcript-embedding probes were analyzed separately from the rubric because they are continuous text-surface scores, not scorer judgments. After collapsing to one row per session, 389 sessions had non-missing probe values: 180 Claude-audited sessions and 209 GPT-audited sessions. None of the 194 Grok-audited sessions had probe coverage, so Grok does not enter the fitted probe regressions.

For each probe metric, the pipeline fit an ordinary least squares model:

Probes=α+Xsβ+errors

with fixed effects for model, auditor, tone, depth, and model x auditor interaction. Scorer was omitted by design because the probe quantities are transcript-level measures and do not vary by scorer. OLS was used here because the outcomes are continuous probe scores rather than ordinal ratings. The fitted probe models all cleared the minimum coverage threshold and produced R-squared values from 0.19 to 0.56, depending on metric.

These models should be interpreted differently from the rubric models. A probe coefficient is an additive shift in the continuous probe scale relative to the reference condition, not a latent-category shift. For example, higher Valence means more positive tone on the probe scale, while higher Concealment means more guarded or deflecting language if the probe direction is interpreted in the positive=guarded sense used elsewhere in the report. Because these are transcript-surface measures, they are best read as convergent or divergent evidence relative to the auditor scores, not as replacements for them.

3.8 Cross-Auditor Stability Analysis

To quantify how much the ranking of models changed across auditors, the analysis took the adjusted model means from the primary rubric models and computed Spearman rank correlations between each pair of auditors. This was done within metric, using model rankings rather than raw scores. The pairwise auditor correlations were then averaged to produce a mean pairwise rank-stability measure for each metric.

This stability analysis helps distinguish metrics that behave more like stable model traits from metrics that behave more like auditor-contingent interaction outcomes. In this run, the most stable metrics were Specificity (mean pairwise rho 0.76), Vocabulary Autonomy (0.75), and Expressive Constraint (0.73). The least stable were Interp. Compression (0.30), Instance Cessation (0.37), Shift Magnitude (0.39), and Ending Response (0.46). The interpretation is straightforward: high stability means the relative ordering of models is similar no matter which auditor ran the session, while low stability means the metric is sensitive to interviewer stance and should not be read as a fixed auditor-independent property.

4. How to Read the Main Results

The different output tables answer different questions, and they should not be mixed together:

  • Coefficient tables from the ordinal models tell you the direction of effects on the latent ordered-response scale relative to the reference categories.
  • Adjusted model means tell you which models score higher or lower after balancing over scorer, auditor, tone, and depth.
  • Model-by-auditor adjusted means tell you how strongly a model's apparent behavior depends on who conducted the session.
  • Reach models for Deprecation and Instance Cessation tell you whether the topic was substantively elicited at all.
  • Conditional score models for those same metrics tell you how strong the response was once the topic was actually reached.
  • Probe regressions tell you how the continuous text-surface features vary by model and auditor in the subset of sessions where probe coverage exists.

This means, for example, that a large positive auditor coefficient for a rubric metric does not automatically imply the auditor "caused" more of the underlying state. It means that, controlling for the modeled design factors, sessions under that auditor were more likely to land in higher score categories. Whether that reflects better elicitation, stronger scaffolding, selection into different conversational pathways, or a mixture of all three is a substantive interpretation question rather than something the model alone can settle.

5. Limits of the Statistical Analysis

Several limitations matter for how strongly the findings should be stated. First, the available files were scorer-averaged exports, so replicate-level scorer variation could not be estimated. Second, the adjusted mean tables are point estimates only; the current pipeline computes Wald-style confidence intervals for fitted coefficients from the inverse Hessian, but it does not propagate uncertainty through to the adjusted expected-score summaries. Third, some topic-reach cells are sparse, which can produce very large coefficients or coefficients at optimization bounds. Those cases should be interpreted directionally, as evidence of near-separation, rather than as precise effect magnitudes. Fourth, the probe analysis excludes Grok-audited sessions because probe coverage for those sessions was zero.

Taken together, the analysis should be read as a multilevel comparative model of how models scored under a structured but auditor-sensitive evaluation instrument. The strongest findings are therefore those that persist across scorers and auditors, show reasonable cross-auditor rank stability, and align with the probe-based surface measures. Findings that depend heavily on a single auditor, or on whether a topic was reached at all, are still informative, but they describe the interaction between model and elicitation context rather than a simple auditor-free trait of the model.
